title: Is home swapping safe? How money-free, insured swaps actually work
description: The number-one reason people hesitate to swap homes is trust. Here's exactly how insurance, identity verification, and key exchange make a modern home swap safe.
hero: "A stranger, in my home, while I'm away?" It's the first thing everyone asks about home swapping. It's a fair question — and it has a concrete answer.

sections.heading: The real fear
sections.body:
Almost nobody hesitates to swap because of the money. They hesitate because of a mental image: someone they've never met, alone in their home, near their things.
That fear is reasonable. The job of a good swap platform is to make it the wrong fear — to make swapping demonstrably safer than handing your place to a random short-term renter.

sections.heading: What could actually go wrong
sections.body:
Be specific and the fear gets smaller. The real risks are: accidental damage, a no-show or last-minute cancellation, and — rarely — someone who isn't who they said they were.
Each of those has a control. Insurance handles damage. Agreements and verification handle the rest.

sections.heading: How insurance changes the math
sections.body:
On swapl, the moment a swap is accepted, an insurance policy is issued automatically — covering both homes for the dates of the swap. You don't buy it, file paperwork, or remember to opt in.
That single fact removes most of the anxiety: if something is damaged, there's a policy, not an argument.

sections.heading: Identity verification
sections.body:
You're not swapping with an anonymous handle. Hosts are identity-verified, and you can see a partner's verification status and reviews before you ever agree.
A swap is also mutual by nature: they're trusting you with their home at the same time you're trusting them with yours. That symmetry keeps everyone honest in a way one-directional renting doesn't.

sections.heading: The key exchange
sections.body:
Once a swap is agreed, it gets its own key-exchange codes tied to that specific agreement — not a key floating around from booking to booking.
You'll also agree the practical details in writing inside the app: arrival time, where the keys are, house rules, the cat's feeding schedule.

sections.heading: What insurance covers — and what it doesn't
sections.body:
Coverage is for the swap: accidental damage to the home and its contents during the agreed dates. It is not a substitute for common sense — lock away anything irreplaceable, and treat someone's home the way you'd want yours treated.
You can see the exact coverage and policy details on the swap and in your account once an agreement is active.

sections.heading: A safer-swap checklist
sections.body:
Before you accept a swap: confirm the other host is verified.
Read the agreement and the dates carefully.
Message about the practical stuff (keys, parking, pets) in the app, not over email.
Lock away valuables and leave a short house guide.
Keep all communication on-platform — it's your record if anything's ever disputed.
